This setting control how a particular Inspection Object Type (IOT) behaves in a specific warehouse number.

This is also very important step, so that IOT is activated for your warehouse.

SCM Extended warehouse management-> Extended warehouse management->Cross process settings->Quality Management->Basics and Integration->Warehouse dependent IOT settings

#4 Maintain Inspection type:

An Inspection Type in SAP Quality Management defines when and how a quality inspection should be performed for a material. It controls inspection lot creation, stock posting, and usage decision processing during business transactions like goods receipt or production. I have created inspection type ZP01 by copying inspection type 1701.

Quality Management->Quality Inspection->Inspection lot creation->Maintain Inspection type

#5 Inspection type for origin

In SAP Quality Management, Inspection Origin identifies the business process that triggered the inspection lot creation. The system uses the inspection origin to determine which inspection type and quality process should be executed.

For EWM integration, inspection origin should always be 17.

Quality Management->Quality Inspection->Inspection lot creation->Inspection type for origin

#6 Default values for the inspection type

Default values of an Inspection Type in SAP Quality Management are predefined settings automatically applied during inspection processing.
These settings control behavior such as automatic inspection lot creation, posting to quality stock, and usage decision requirements.

Quality Management->Quality Inspection->Inspection lot creation-> Default values for the inspection type

#7 Define Follow-up actions for inspection results

Define Follow-Up Actions for Inspection Results in SAP Extended Warehouse Management controls what action the system should perform after Usage Decision (UD). It determines activities such as stock movement, warehouse task creation, and posting stock to unrestricted or blocked stock after inspection completion.

SCM Extended warehouse management-> Extended warehouse management->Cross process settings->Quality Management->Inspection results ->Maintain follow-up actions

#8 Code group for Usage Decision

A Code Group for Usage Decision in SAP Quality Management is a collection of decision codes used during Usage Decision processing. It groups multiple UD codes such as Accept, Reject, Rework, or Scrap under one logical category for inspection processing.

Quality Management->Quality Inspection->Inspection lot completion-> Edit code groups and codes for usage decision

#9 Selected set codes for Usage Decision:

Selected Set Codes for Usage Decision in SAP Quality Management are predefined Usage Decision codes grouped together for inspection processing.
They allow users to select standardized decisions such as Accept, Reject, Rework, or Scrap during UD execution.

As per my personal experience, I missed below configuration and I was not able to see any UD code, that has eaten significant time.

Quality Management->Quality Inspection->Inspection lot completion-> Selected set codes for Usage decision

#10 Maintain Settings at plant level

Maintain Settings at Plant Level in SAP Quality Management is used to define quality management control settings specific to a plant. It controls behavior such as inspection lot creation, usage decision processing, stock posting, and quality integration for materials within that plant.

Quality Management->Basic Settings->Maintain Settings at plant level

Master data:

#1 Material Master – QM view:

Inspection type ZP04(created above) is assigned to material) under quality view using MM01. inspection Type is assigned to the material master in SAP Quality Management so the system knows when and how quality inspection should be triggered for that specific material. It controls inspection lot creation, inspection stock posting, and quality processing during activities such as goods receipt, production, or warehouse movements. Without assigning an inspection type, SAP will not know that the material requires quality inspection.

#2 Maintain Inspection rule(/SCWM/QRSETUP):

Maintain Inspection Rule in SAP Extended Warehouse Management defines how the system should create and process inspections during warehouse activities.
It links warehouse number, inspection type, inspection object type (IOT), follow-up actions, and stock processing behaviour together.
The inspection rule controls when inspection documents are created and how stock is handled after Usage Decision (UD).

End to end Transactions:

#1 Inbound delivery creation(VL31N):

Inbound delivery is created with reference to purchase order and this should be distributed to EWM system. I am not using Handling unit(HU) in this example.

#2 Warehouse task creation(/SCWM/PRDI):

As a material receipt process, warehouse task needs to be created. And Inspection lot # 170000002613.

#3 Warehouse task confirmation (/SCWM/MON):

This step, warehouse task is confirmed.

 

 

#4 Warehouse stock(/SCWM/MON):

Below is EWM warehouse stock before quality stock

 

#5 Storage location stock(MMBE):

Below is storage location stock before quality check.

 

#6 Result recording(QA32):

User do record inspection characteristics such as measurements, defects, or test outcomes before completing the Usage Decision (UD). It helps SAP determine whether the material should be accepted, rejected, or sent for rework.

Select the Result recording

Entered the results

 

After Result recording, update UD code “A1” and then select “EWM Inspection”

 

Entered decision as “A01” and follow-up action as “EWM_A”

 

 

#7 Warehouse stock(/SCWM/MON):

After quality test, result recording and UD, EWM warehouse stock moved to F2(Un restricted)

 

#8 Storage location stock(MMBE):

Below is storage location stock after quality stock, Quality stocks has been moved to Unrestricted stock.

 

Conclusion:

The integration between SAP Extended Warehouse Management and SAP Quality Management in SAP S/4HANA provides a powerful framework for synchronizing warehouse execution and quality inspection processes within modern supply chain operations. Through proper configuration of inspection object types, inspection rules, inspection types, usage decision settings, and warehouse-dependent controls, organizations can achieve seamless quality inspection processing integrated directly with warehouse movements and stock management.

As demonstrated in this document, the end-to-end process starting from inbound delivery creation, warehouse task execution, inspection lot processing, result recording, and usage decision execution ensures accurate stock visibility, better traceability, and improved operational control across EWM-managed warehouses. A well-designed EWM-QM integration significantly improves inventory accuracy, inspection efficiency, compliance management, and overall warehouse productivity while reducing manual intervention and process inconsistencies.

 

Author: Naveen Chandra Sharma

(Naveen is SAP Solution architect having 29+ years of experience which includes 20+ years into SAP consulting with expertise in SAP ECC, SAP S/4HANA and integration with other external system like MES, CIMCORP etc. He worked on various type of project flavours like Implementation, Enhancement and support projects in various industries Manufacturing, Finance, Pharmaceutical, Food, Telecommunication)

entry.title: SAP EWM Migration to S/4HANA: Advanced Capabilities for Carve-Out and Consolidation Scenarios
entry.content:
SAP EWM on Business Suite to SAP EWM on SAP S/4HANA: A proven solution, Now even more powerful

As organizations accelerate their journey to SAP S/4HANA, warehouse management systems face a critical challenge: how to migrate without disrupting daily operations. The standard SAP Migration Cockpit approach requires closing all EWM transactional data and new start on EWM on S/4HANA  - a significant constraint that can extend project timelines and impact business continuity.

In 2020, SAP's Center of Expertise for EWM (CoE EWM) and the Data Management and Landscape Transformation (DMLT) developed an innovative transactional data migration approach specifically designed for customers who did not wish a complete reimplementation of existing EWM solution. This solution enables the migration of an entire classic EWM system to EWM on S/4HANA, including all related transaction data regardless of status. Learn more about the original approach here.

Since its introduction, this solution has been successfully deployed in over 5+ production environments across industries including retail, manufacturing, and logistics (2020-2024).

Now, as of 2025, we're taking this solution further.

What's New: Advanced Migration Capabilities

The proven migration approach has been enhanced by MAX (fka. CoE) and DMLT to support three powerful new scenarios that go beyond basic system-to-system migration:

  • Carve-out: Selective warehouse extraction
  • Consolidation: Merging into existing systems
  • Hybrid: Combining both strategies

These capabilities enable not just technical migration, but true business transformation—allowing you to reshape your warehouse system landscape to align with current and future business needs.

Understanding the Core Solution

Before exploring the advanced scenarios, let's recap what makes this migration approach unique:

Key Characteristics

  • One-to-one data transfer: Complete migration of all EWM related transactional data 
  • Status-independent: Migration open warehouse task, delivery documents, handling units, and stock data without requiring closure.
  • Business continuity: Enables uninterrupted warehouse operations throughout the migration
  • Comprehensive scope: Includes all EWM-related objects: warehouse structures, resources, processes, and historical data

What Gets Migrated 

  • Warehouse structure and configuration
  • Stock data (all statuses and types)
  • Open and completed warehouse tasks
  • Active handling units
  • Warehouse requests (Inbound and outbound documents)
  • Resource management data
  • Queue assignments and work packages
  • Quality inspection documents

What does NOT get migrated

  • Master Data
    Master data must be distributed from ERP System to the EWM System
  • Customizing
    Customizing  This must be transported via customizing request into the target system

Migration Scenarios: Choose Your Strategic Path

Carve out: selective warehouse extraction

What it is:

Selectively migrate one or more complete warehouses from your classic EWM system into a clean (greenfield) S/4HANA environment. The carve-out occurs at the warehouse level, ensuring all related data moves together as a cohesive unit.

Warehouse carve-out

Ideal for:

  • Divestitures: Separating business units or facilities for sale or spin-off
  • Regional separation: Creating dedicated systems for specific geographies
  • Legal entity splits: Establishing independent systems for regulatory or organizational reasons
  • Organizational restructuring: Aligning IT systems with new business structures

Business Benefits:

  • Clean system boundaries for independent operations
  • Reduced complexity in remaining source system
  • Facilitates organizational changes 
  • Optimized licensing and operational costs

Consolidation: Merging into existing systems

What it is:

Migrate your entire classic EWM system and merge it into an existing (brownfield) S/4HANA EWM environment. This approach combines multiple EWM systems into a single, unified platform.

Warehouse consolidation

Ideal for:

  • System landscape simplification: Reducing the number of EWM instances
  • Post-merger integration: Combining warehouse systems after acquisitions
  • Standardization initiatives: Creating a single source of truth for warehouse operations
  • IT cost reduction: Minimizing infrastructure and maintenance overhead

Business Benefits:

  • Reduced total cost of ownership (TCO)
  • Simplified IT landscape management
  • Enhanced visibility across all warehouse operations
  • Streamlined support and maintenance
  • Easier implementation of global standards

Hybrid: combining both strategies

What it is:

Selectively extract specific warehouses from a source classic EWM system (carve-out) and integrate them into an existing S/4HANA EWM environment (consolidation). This combines the best of both approaches.

Warehouse carve-out and consolidation

 

Ideal for:

  • Complex reorganizations: system consolidation
  • Phased transformations: Gradual migration of selected facilities
  • Multi-company structures: Aligning systems with complex organizational hierarchies

Business Benefits:

  • Maximum flexibility in system design
  • Supports complex business transformation strategies
  • Enables incremental change with controlled risk
  • Optimizes both technical architecture and business operations

From Migration to Transformation: Unlocking Strategic Value

In addition to the benefits outlined in the previous blog, these advanced capabilities extend far beyond technical system migration -they enable authentic business transformation:

1. Warehouse Landscape Redesign

  • Align system architecture with business strategy
  • Optimize for current operational realities
  • Build flexibility for future growth and change

2. System Consolidation & Simplification

  • Reduce the number of EWM instances
  • Lower IT complexity and operational overhead
  • Streamline support and reduce maintenance costs

3. Improved Scalability

  • Built-in capacity for business growth
  • Easier integration of new warehouses or acquisitions
  • Future-ready architecture on S/4HANA platform

4. Enhanced Visibility & Control

  • Single source of truth for inventory and operations
  • Improved reporting and analytics capabilities
  • Better decision-making with consolidated data

Next Steps: Start Your Migration Journey

Ready to explore how these advanced migration capabilities can support your S/4HANA transformation?

Get Started:

  1. Assessment: Schedule a consultation to evaluate your current EWM landscape and identify the optimal migration scenario
  2. Strategy: Work with our experts to design your target architecture and migration roadmap
  3. Proof of Concept: Validate the approach with a pilot warehouse or limited scope
  4. Full Implementation: Execute the comprehensive migration with expert support

Contact Us:

For more information about the advanced EWM migration approach or to discuss your specific requirements, please contact:

📧 Email: EWM Migration Consultant

entry.title: SAP Transportation Management ( TM) Reports for Logistics Integration
entry.content:
Reports for Logistics Integration

This overview lists reports available in the logistics integration area of Transportation Management (TM). These reports assist with integration and post-integration processes between TM and Sales and Distribution (SD), Materials Management (MM), Logistics Execution (LE), and nJIT (Just-In-Time).

1. Report to Check Integration for SO/PO/Delivery and FU

Technical Name: /SCMTMS/CHK_DOC_INT_TM_INTEGR

2. Activation of Purchase Scheduling Agreements Without Delivery Integration

  • Technical Name: /SCMTMS/ACTIVATE_MMSAG_WO_DLV
  • Description: Run to activate the integration of purchase scheduling agreements without integrating the corresponding inbound deliveries.

3. Activation of Documents for Transportation Management

  • Technical Name: /SCMTMS/LI_ACTIVATE
  • Description: Run to activate the integration of business transaction documents into TM in an internal integration setup.

4. Adjustment of Integration of Purchase Scheduling Agreements Without Delivery Integration

  • Technical Name: /SCMTMS/ADAPT_START_MM_WO_DLV
  • Description: Run to adjust the integration start date and reduction quantity for a purchase scheduling agreement item without delivery integration.

5. Create Deliveries in ERP

  • Technical Name: /SCMTMS/DLV_BATCH
  • Description: Run to create deliveries based on existing freight units.

6. Deactivation of Purchase Scheduling Agreements

  • Technical Name: /SCMTMS/DEACTIVATE_MMSAG_W_DLV
  • Description: Run to deactivate your purchase scheduling agreements.

7. Prepone End Dates for Items in Purchase Scheduling Agreement

  • Technical Name: /SCMTMS/UPDATE_INT_TIMERANGE
  • Description: Run to verify end dates and reschedule them for an earlier date.

8. Reactivation of Purchase Scheduling Agreements

  • Technical Name: /SCMTMS/REACTIVATE_MMSAG_W_DLV
  • Description: Run to reactivate the integration of purchase scheduling agreements into TM.

9. Setting and Fixing of Integration Time Range End Date for Purchase Scheduling Agreements

  • Technical Name: /SCMTMS/FIX_INT_TIMERANGE
  • Description: Run to set a fixed end date for the end of the integration of purchase scheduling agreements.

10. Subsequent Freight Unit Building in Internal TM Component Integration

  • Technical Name: /SCMTMS/SUBSEQENT_FUB_LOGINT
  • Description: Run to trigger the creation of freight unit in an internal TM component integration setup.

11. Update of Purchase Scheduling Agreements Based on TM Planning Results

  • Technical Name: /SCMTMS/TM_SAG_PLANNING_SYNC
  • Description: Run to synchronize the transportation planning date of freight unit items with the delivery date of the corresponding schedule line in the purchase scheduling agreement.

entry.title: Combined Picking in SAP EWM for Multiple Deliveries with Common Products
entry.content:
Combined Picking in SAP EWM for Multiple Deliveries with Common Products

Introduction

In high-volume warehouse environments, it is common to receive multiple outbound deliveries containing identical products for different customers.
Executing picking separately for each outbound delivery often results in:

  • excessive warehouse task creation,

  • repeated travel paths,

  • unnecessary picker movement,

  • increased labor effort,

  • and reduced warehouse efficiency.

SAP EWM provides the capability to optimize this process using Combined Picking, where products from multiple outbound deliveries are consolidated into a single warehouse order for execution.

This article demonstrates a practical warehouse execution scenario involving:

  • 3 outbound deliveries,

  • common products across all deliveries,

  • single combined warehouse order creation,

  • consolidated warehouse task execution,

  • and final distribution back to respective deliveries.

Business Scenario

A warehouse receives 3 outbound deliveries for different customers:

Customer Outbound Delivery

Walmart SuperCenter80010771
Costco Wholesale80010772
Albertsons80010773

All 3 deliveries contain the same products:

  • Product 1

  • Product 2

  • Product 3

Instead of generating separate picking tasks for each delivery, SAP EWM combines the picking quantities into a single warehouse execution process.

Delivery Overview

Delivery 1 – Walmart SuperCenter

Material Description Delivery Qty

250054Product 11 EA
250055Product 22 EA
250056Product 33 EA

Delivery 2 – Costco Wholesale

Material Description Delivery Qty

250054Product 14 EA
250055Product 25 EA
250056Product 36 EA

Delivery 3 – Albertsons

Material Description Delivery Qty

250054Product 17 EA
250055Product 28 EA
250056Product 39 EA

Combined Warehouse Order Creation

SAP EWM combines all 9 delivery items into a single warehouse order in queue:

EACHESPICK

Instead of:

  • 9 individual warehouse tasks,

  • multiple travel paths,

  • and repetitive picking,

the system intelligently consolidates quantities for common products.

This significantly improves:

  • picker productivity,

  • travel optimization,

  • warehouse throughput,

  • and labor efficiency.

Combined Warehouse Tasks

The warehouse picker now executes combined picking quantities:

Material Description Total Qty to Pick UoM

250054Product 112EA
250055Product 215EA
250056Product 318EA

Total:

45 EA across 3 products

The picker performs picking in a single wave instead of executing repetitive delivery-based picks.

Picking Execution

During execution:

  • picker retrieves consolidated quantities,

  • products are staged temporarily,

  • quantities are later sorted/distributed back to respective outbound deliveries,

  • and final loading is executed customer-wise.

This approach minimizes:

  • repeated aisle movement,

  • RF scanning effort,

  • and warehouse congestion.

Distribution Back to Deliveries

After combined picking execution, quantities are redistributed back to the original deliveries.

Material Walmart Costco Albertsons Total Picked

Product 11 EA4 EA7 EA12 EA
Product 22 EA5 EA8 EA15 EA
Product 33 EA6 EA9 EA18 EA

This ensures:

  • customer-level delivery integrity,

  • accurate outbound processing,

  • and optimized warehouse execution simultaneously.

Operational Benefits

Reduced Warehouse Travel Time

Picker visits storage bins once for combined quantities instead of repeating trips per delivery.

Improved Warehouse Productivity

Fewer warehouse tasks and consolidated execution improve throughput.

Lower RF Interaction Effort

Reduced scanning and confirmation steps for warehouse operators.

Better Wave Optimization

Combined picking aligns well with wave management and outbound staging strategies.

Reduced Queue Volume

Warehouse order consolidation minimizes queue and task fragmentation.

Important Design Considerations

Before implementing combined picking, warehouses should evaluate:

  • customer segregation requirements,

  • staging space availability,

  • sorting effort after picking,

  • handling unit strategies,

  • transportation/loading constraints,

  • and warehouse process type alignment.

Combined picking is especially effective for:

  • retail distribution,

  • wholesale fulfillment,

  • FTL outbound scenarios,

  • and high-volume repetitive product movements.

Key Takeaways

This scenario demonstrates how SAP EWM can intelligently optimize outbound execution by consolidating common products across multiple deliveries into a single warehouse execution flow.

Process Summary:

  • 3 deliveries

  • 3 customers

  • Common products across all deliveries

  • 1 combined warehouse order

  • 45 EA picked in a consolidated wave

  • Quantities redistributed back to original deliveries

The result is:

higher warehouse efficiency, reduced picker movement, optimized labor utilization, and improved outbound execution performance.

Conclusion

Combined Picking in SAP EWM is not merely a technical warehouse task consolidation feature — it is a warehouse execution optimization strategy.

When designed correctly, it can significantly improve:

  • warehouse throughput,

  • labor productivity,

  • picking efficiency,

  • and operational scalability in high-volume distribution environments.

As warehouses continue moving toward larger fulfillment volumes and tighter outbound timelines, combined picking becomes an increasingly valuable execution strategy within SAP EWM.

entry.title: Returnable Packaging Materials in SAP EWM - Closed Loop
entry.content:
Introduction

Reusable materials, often called Returnable Transport Packaging (RTP), are an important part of modern supply chains. In industries such as consumer packaged goods (CPG), automotive, retail, life sciences, and industrial manufacturing, companies use assets like pallets, crates, totes, racks, drums, and containers to move goods efficiently and safely.

In CPG, especially food and beverage, examples include CHEP pallets, Euro pallets, and totes. These materials help products move smoothly across suppliers, plants, warehouses, and retailers. Similar models are used in other industries to improve handling, reduce waste, and support more consistent operations.

Many companies prefer reusable packaging over single-use alternatives because it can reduce cost, improve asset utilization, and support sustainability goals. In some cases, these assets are leased or pooled through providers such as CHEP, RPC, and Euro Pool System, giving companies access to shared packaging networks without having to own all the assets themselves.

CHEP pallet

The business model often depends on who owns the packaging:

Ownership model What it means
Customer-ownedThe customer owns the packaging materials and provides them to suppliers as needed.
Service provider-ownedA logistics or pooling provider owns the materials and makes them available for a rental or usage fee.
Supplier-ownedThe supplier owns the packaging used to deliver products to the customer.

SAP Returnable Packaging Management

SAP Returnable Packaging Management(SAP RPM) is a cloud-based solution that helps companies track, plan, and manage returnable packaging materials across closed-loop supply chain operations.

Closed-loop packaging operations enable companies to move goods in reusable containers that are returned, tracked, and redeployed across the supply chain, improving visibility, reducing waste, and supporting more efficient packaging management.

Without SAP RPM

Even without SAP Returnable Packaging Management, organizations can design a practical closed-loop packaging process in SAP S/4HANA and EWM by defining returnable packaging materials, managing them as special stock( V - Customer Returnable Packaging Stock). 

This setup demonstrates how returnable packaging moves in a closed loop between shipper and customer.

Goods Receipt, Goods Issue, and Returns Process for Returnable Packaging

 

Goods Receipt Process

In this example, materials are externally procured from suppliers or stocks received from other plants, and delivered in reusable packaging materials such as pallets, bins, or containers. The objective is to show how the reusable packaging can be received, tracked, and managed in SAP S/4HANA and EWM as part of a closed-loop process.

Prerequisites

In the Embedded/decentralized EWM system, activate the required Business Configuration Sets using transaction SCPR20. In particular, activate BC Set /SCWM/DLV_INBOUND_PACK to create the necessary item types and related settings required for packaging material line creation in inbound deliveries.

Master Data

a. Define packaging materials in SAP S/4HANA

Create the reusable packaging materials in SAP S/4HANA. Depending on the business requirement, these materials may represent serialized or non-serialized containers.

Material Type - VERP

 

b. Define EWM Goods Movement Bin

Create a virtual storage bin to receive and issue these packaging materials. In this example, the bin PACK_MATERIAL is set up and assigned for goods movement bin determination using transaction /SCWM/GMBIN_DET.

 

Configuration

EWM

a. Activate Automatic Packaging Item Generation for Inbound Delivery Item Types

SPRO path: SCM Extended Warehouse Management → Extended Warehouse Management → Goods Receipt Process → Automatically Generate Packaging for Inbound Deliveries

 

b. Copy the standard status profile /SCWM/INB_PRD_PAC to a custom status profile. This is required to deactivate status type DPT (Putaway), which avoids an additional putaway step for the packaging material and ensures goods receipt bin determination is driven via /SCWM/GMBIN_DET.

SPRO path: SCM Extended Warehouse Management → Extended Warehouse Management → Cross-Process Settings → Delivery-Warehouse Request → Define Status Profiles

c. Assign the custom status profile to the IPAC item type.

SPRO path: SCM Extended Warehouse Management → Extended Warehouse Management → Goods Receipt Process → Inbound Delivery → Define Item Types for Inbound Delivery Process

 

d. Maintain the packaging material type and the assigned number range type.

SPRO path: SCM Extended Warehouse Management → Extended Warehouse Management → Cross-Process Settings → Handling Units → Define Packaging Material Types for Warehousing

Goods Issue Process

In this example, I considered a scenario where reusable packaging materials are shipped to the customer.

Prerequisites

In the decentralized EWM system, activate the required BC Sets using transaction SCPR20, and activate BC set /SCWM/DLV_OUTBOUND_PACK to create the necessary item types and related configuration to support packaging material line creation in outbound deliveries.

Master Data

EWM

a. Define the EWM Goods Movement Bin

In /SCWM/GMBIN_DET, assign storage bin PACK_MATERIAL to item type OPHU.

Configuration

a. Activate packaging items generation for Item type

SCM Extended Warehouse Management -> Extended Warehouse Management -> Goods Issue Process -> Outbound Delivery -> Automatically Generate Packaging Items

b. Activate Automatic Packing in Delivery Type - S4

SPRO path: Logistics Execution → Shipping → Deliveries → Define Delivery Types

c. Maintain Item Category Determination - S4

SPRO path: Logistics Execution → Shipping → Deliveries → Define Item Categories for Deliveries

 

d. Maintain Item Category Copy Routines for Delivery Type–Sales Order Type Combinations - S4

SPRO path: Logistics Execution → Shipping → Copying Control → Specify Copy Control for Deliveries


 

Define Schedule Line Category 

Assign Schedule Line Category to Item Category

System Demo

 

Goods Receipt Process

I created a purchase order and delivery in S/4, then distributed the inbound delivery to EWM. During goods receipt in EWM, a packaging material line item is generated automatically on the inbound delivery.

Purchase order Creation

PO# 4500017291

Inbound Delivery # 180001104

EWM Inbound Delivery

Pack and post goods receipt. New packaging line item should get added in the inbound delivery.

I also posted Inventory for Packaging Material PKE-090

Verify the stock in warehouse monitor, inbound delivery should be blank in the stock

Verify S4 delivery for packaging material line item addition

Goods Issue Process

I created a sales order and delivery in S/4, then distributed the outbound delivery to EWM. During goods issue in EWM, a packaging material line item is generated automatically on the outbound delivery.

Sales Order # 15553

Outbound Delivery # 80120258

EWM Outbound Delivery # 310121

Pick and post goods issue for this delivery to add the packaging material line item. I packed 2 HUs using 2 different packaging materials.

Stock in warehouse monitor

 
 
Once the material is issued to the customer, the system creates an entry like the one shown below. It then posts movement type 621, transferring the material from unrestricted stock to special stock.

Returnable Packaging Pickup

To illustrate the returns process, I created a returns sales order with order type AT for packaging material PKE-090, which is returned by the customer and received back into warehouse stock.

 

Create Returns Delivery

 Movement type in this case would be 622 

Return Delivery # 84000046 

Post Goods Receipt in EWM

 

Refresh Stock Overview

Special Stock will move to unrestricted stock

This approach helps you track packaging inventory at the customer site and request its return when required. If the customer decides to retain the packaging material, it can be billed using sales order type RPI.

Use the MB58 report for reporting and inventory tracking.

For rental items, this information can also be shared with leasing companies such as CHEP and IFCO through file transfers, IDocs, or other integration mechanisms.

Thanks for reading this blog post.

References:

Generation of Delivery Items for Packaging Materials | SAP Help Portal

Learn SAP skills | SAP Learning

entry.title: Are You Chasing "Ghost Stock"? The Reality Check Your Warehouse Needs
entry.content:
Are You Chasing "Ghost Stock"? The Reality Check Your Warehouse Needs

Manyat Thinking: Simplicity is the Ultimate Sophistication

Introduction

In my day-to-day experience managing warehouse operations and handling logistics flows, nothing frustrates an execution team more than a system showing stock that physically doesn't exist on the floor.

You check the bin… empty.
You check the system… it insists the stock is right there.

We all know this operational headache as Ghost Stock. Whenever I discuss these visibility discrepancies with warehouse managers, the instant reaction is often to blame an SAP bug or ask for a complex system enhancement. But if you look at it through the lens of "Manyat Thinking"—where we believe simplicity is always better than over-engineering—the issue is rarely a configuration failure. It is almost always a gap in the physical movement process.

The Shopkeeper Paradox: Understanding Ghost Stock

To understand why Ghost Stock occurs even in a massive automated warehouse, we don't need to look at complex system architecture first. We just need to look at a local shopkeeper.

Imagine a shopkeeper who receives a fresh shipment of goods. On Day 1, he does a physical count, verifies everything, and stores the items on his shelves. But a few days later, during a routine stock check, he notices a mismatch. The physical items on the shelf do not match what he thinks should be there. Suddenly, he is dealing with "Ghost Stock"—inventory that exists in his mind or old notes, but is physically missing from the shelf.

What does he do to solve this? He doesn't panic; he executes a manual supply chain audit:

- He checks the original invoice to see what actually entered his shop. (In SAP EWM, this is your PRDI — Product Inbound Delivery).
- He reviews his pending and confirmed orders to see what was moved or misplaced within the shop. (In SAP EWM, this is your ORDIM_C — Warehouse Task/Confirmation).
- He tracks what has already been sold and packed for the customers. (In SAP EWM, this is your PRDO — Product Outbound Delivery).

By matching these three points, the shopkeeper instantly finds his mistake. He realizes exactly which stock was pending, which was confirmed, and which had already left the shop.

The biggest realization here is this: Whether it is a small retail shopkeeper or a 500,000 sq. ft. automated distribution center running SAP EWM, the foundational logic of supply chain visibility remains exactly the same. Ghost stock isn't a system glitch; it is a breakdown in tracking the execution journey between Inbound, Internal Movement, and Outbound.

The Reconciliation Logic: Follow the Flow

Instead of just staring at system screens or waiting for custom reports, the real solution lies in understanding exactly how data flows across the execution chain. If you look at the reconciliation diagram I attached with this post, you can see how we map warehouse behavior across those same three critical checkpoints.

1. /SCWM/PRDI — Planned Expectation
An Inbound Delivery is not just a document. It represents the expected movement.
- What material is supposed to arrive
- How much quantity is expected
This is the starting point of warehouse visibility.

2. /SCWM/ORDIM_C — Execution Reality
The truth lies in confirmed Warehouse Tasks.
- Confirmed tasks represent actual execution
- Unconfirmed tasks indicate incomplete movement
For example, if 10 units were expected but only 8 were confirmed, those remaining 2 units were never fully processed in the system. This is where inconsistencies begin.

3. /SCWM/PRDO — Final Result
Outbound Delivery reflects what actually left the warehouse.
- What was picked
- What was issued
Any mismatch here exposes hidden process issues.

The Manyat Formula for Clarity
Inbound = Intent
Tasks = Execution
Outbound = Result

When these three layers remain aligned, warehouse visibility stays accurate.

Gap Analysis: Where the Ghost Is Created
Ghost Stock is usually created because of:
- Wrong postings
- Incomplete confirmations
- Process gaps
- Data inconsistencies

In most cases, the issue is not the stock itself. The issue is the break in the movement chain.

Key Insight: Practical Action Playbook for Reducing Ghost Stock

Theoretical understanding is important, but operational control is what actually reduces Ghost Stock in day-to-day warehouse execution. Below is a simple operational approach that warehouse teams can use to identify and reduce visibility gaps.

1. Daily Triad Validation
Perform a quick comparison between /SCWM/PRDI (Expected), /SCWM/ORDIM_C (Confirmed), and /SCWM/PRDO (Issued). Any mismatch between these three layers should immediately be reviewed delivery-wise. This helps identify visibility breaks before they grow into larger inventory inconsistencies.

2. Define Clear Exception Buckets
Warehouse teams should classify mismatches into clear categories such as:
- Short Confirmation (Expected quantity > Confirmed quantity)
- Over Confirmation (Confirmed quantity > Expected quantity)
- Issue Gap (Confirmed quantity ≠ Issued quantity)
Once these categories are standardized, corrective actions become much faster and more consistent.

3. Root Cause Classification
Each mismatch should be tagged with a clear operational reason:
- Incomplete Warehouse Task
- Wrong bin posting
- Queue or timing delay
- User execution error
Over time, these classifications reveal recurring operational patterns.

4. Fast Correction Process
A simple correction workflow can significantly reduce Ghost Stock duration:
- Open Warehouse Tasks → Complete or cancel appropriately
- Bin mismatch → Recount and adjust inventory
- Outbound gap → Reconcile picking and goods issue activities
Speed of correction is critical for maintaining warehouse visibility accuracy.

5. Prevention Controls
Strong process controls reduce future inconsistencies:
- RF validations for quantity, batch, and bin
- Mandatory task confirmations
- Cut-off validations before Goods Issue
- Additional checks for high-value materials
Prevention is always more effective than repeated correction.

6. Simple KPI Monitoring
Operational visibility improves when warehouses track measurable indicators such as:
- Mismatch percentage between PRDI and ORDIM_C
- Issue gap percentage between ORDIM_C and PRDO
- Average resolution time for mismatches

Ghost Stock is not a system failure. It is a visibility issue created by incomplete movement execution. Where movement stops, visibility becomes unreliable.

My Personal Take: Movement Over Screens

In managing and designing warehouse flows, I firmly believe that technology, systems, and even AI should support smarter operational decisions—not simply automate a weak, messy, or overcomplicated process.

When you synchronize your yard visibility, staging logic, and cross-docking properly, complexity drops naturally and your inventory stays clean.

In SAP EWM, stock is not just a static quantity. It is about movement, confirmation, and control.

Conclusion

Counting stock is easy. Understanding how that stock moved is what defines a real warehouse professional. Because in SAP EWM, stock is not just about quantity. It is about movement, confirmation, and control. And where movement becomes incomplete, Ghost Stock begins to appear.

I would also love to hear how different warehouse teams identify and control Ghost Stock situations in real-world operations. Let’s share some practical fixes in the comments.

— Rahul Kumar
Manyat Thinking | Part of Manyat Project – SAP EWM Simplified

entry.title: Changing the unit of measure during RF packing in SAP EWM
entry.content:
Introduction

In material and engineering manufacturing industries, products are often managed and sold in more than one unit of measure. A typical example is steel bars: stocked and sold by length (1 m, 2 m, 3 m, etc.), yet some customers require delivery in KG, or warehouse teams need to repack stock into KG before outbound shipment.

SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM) supports this scenario as a standard operation through the RF/mobile device packing transaction - no custom development required. This blog walks through the end-to-end configuration and testing steps.

Business scenario

  • Product: Steel Bar
  • Base / ordering UoM: M (metres)
  • Alternative UoM: KG

Requirement: During outbound packing on the RF device, the operator repacks a quantity of steel bars into a destination HU expressed in KG

Define Unit of Measurement Attributes (prerequisite)

This is a critical step that is easy to overlook. For the UoM switch to work during RF packing, the alternative unit must be flagged as Stock-Specific UoM at the warehouse level.

IMG path: EWM > Master Data > Product > Define Unit of Measurement Attributes

Define Unit of Measurement Attributes - KG flagged as Stock-Specific UoM for warehouse

Create Exception code

IMG path: EWM > Cross-Process settings > Exception Handling > Create Exception Codes

  • Warehouse:XXXX
  • Exception Code: AUOM (or any 4-character key, e.g. ZUOM, XAUO)
  • Description: Change AUoM

     

    Assign the business context

    Navigate to child menu Define Exception Code > Details and set the following:

    • Business Context: PIM – Packing/Unpacking Products (Internal Movement)
    • Execution Step: A0 (background)
    • Exception Code Profile: assign only if restricting to a specific user group


Exception Code details - Business Context: PIM Packing/Unpacking, Execution Step: A0

Within the exception code configuration, maintain the process parameter for differences. Set Difference Category to blank - this ensures no stock difference is posted to a bin, account, or delivery when the UoM is switched. The system converts the quantity using the material master conversion factor.

Assign Internal Exception Code to your Exception Code

Navigate to:  IMG > EWM > Cross-process setting > Exception Handling > Assign Internal Exception Codes to Exception Codes

Create a new entry mapping your external exception code to SAP's delivered internal exception code AUOM - "Change Quantity and/or Unit". This is the link that allows EWM to trigger the UoM change function automatically when the exception is raised during RF packing. Assign Internal Exception Codes - external code mapped to internal AUOM (Change Quantity and/or Unit)

Testing - RF device

Navigate to: RFUI > Outbound Process > Packing > Manually Repack HU Item

Scan the source HU RF device - scan the source HU containing the steel bars

Select item and switch UoM

The system displays the item (Steel Bar for Export, 3 M). Press SF2 (UoMC) - the UoM field opens and becomes editable. Change to KG and enter the required quantity (e.g. 5 KG). RF device - press SF2 (UoMC) to open the UoM field; item currently shows 3 M


Assign destination HU

Enter the destination HU (pre-created, or create in real time using F4 HUCrt). The screen now confirms AQty = 5 KG with the destination HU and storage bin. 

 RF device - destination HU assigned, quantity confirmed as 5 KG

Result - HU overview

After confirming, the HU overview shows the stock correctly split across both handling units:HU overview - source HU retains remaining metres; destination HU holds 5 KG


Desktop packing transaction

The same UoM conversion is available in the /SCWM/PACK desktop work center (Work Center Pack in Goods Issue) without any additional exception code configuration. The Alt. Unit and Quantity columns are available directly in the packing item list.

 /SCWM/PACK desktop transaction - UoM conversion available with no extra configuration

 

Summary

This standard SAP EWM capability is particularly relevant for industries handling dual-unit materials: steel, aluminium, wire, timber, fabric, bulk chemicals, and similar products.

  • Enable Stock-Specific UoM for the alternative unit in warehouse settings - this is the key prerequisite
  • Configure an exception code mapped to SAP's internal AUOM exception
  • Warehouse operators can switch UoM directly on the RF device during packing
  • No stock discrepancies are posted - the system converts using the material master factor

The same operation is also available in the /SCWM/PACK desktop transaction with no extra config

I hope this guide is helpful. Open for feedback and suggestions - please share your experience or questions in the comments below.

entry.title: SAP EWM–PP Integration: PMR-Based Staging, Consumption, and Finished Goods Receipt
entry.content:
Abstract & Introduction:

In modern manufacturing environments, seamless integration between production planning and warehouse execution is essential for ensuring efficient material flow, inventory accuracy, and real-time operational visibility. SAP S/4HANA provides this capability through the integration of SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM) and Production Planning (PP), enabling synchronized execution of production supply and warehouse processes.

This article presents an end-to-end overview of SAP EWM and PP integration, covering both system configuration and business process execution. The paper explains key integration components such as production supply areas (PSAs), production material requests (PMRs), staging methods, warehouse task creation, material consumption, production confirmations, and finished goods receipt processing. It also highlights the interaction between production orders and warehouse activities, demonstrating how materials are staged to production lines and how finished products are received and managed within EWM.

Objective is to provide both functional consultants and business users with a practical understanding of how SAP EWM and PP collaborate to support efficient and integrated manufacturing operations.

Production supply area(PSA):
A production supply area is the location where components are staged and made available for production consumption. Its purpose is to Supplies components to production and Acts as the destination for EWM staging.

Control Cycle:
A control cycle defines how a specific material is supplied from the warehouse to a specific PSA and controls the staging process for production.

Material + PSA + Plant -> Staging Method

SAP PP/EWM, Control Cycles are classified based on the staging method used to supply components to the PSA.

1. Pick Parts Staging: Components are staged for a specific production order and picked exactly according to the order requirement.
2. Release Order Parts Staging: Components are staged automatically when the production order is released.
3. Crate Parts Staging: Materials are replenished in the PSA based on minimum and maximum quantities instead of production order demand.

In SAP EWM Advanced Production Integration (API), the control cycle can be configured with EWM Staging. In this case, EWM controls the staging process through PMR (Production Material Request).

  1. Single-Order Staging: Materials are staged specifically for an individual production order.
  2. Cross-Order Staging: Materials are staged collectively for multiple production orders instead of one specific order.

Under this document, I am trying to make a scenario for a finish material # 1001590 and having three components.

#1 1001575 – EWM staging (Single order release)

#2 1001596 – Replenishment of Crate parts

#3 1001597 – Manual staging

Customization:

SAP S/4HANA

#1 Maintain Storage location(SLOC)

This is an organizational unit within a plant where materials are physically stored and inventory is managed. it belongs to exactly one plant and represents a physical or logical storage area. This is the lowest inventory management level in ERP/MM where stock quantities are maintained.

SPRO->Enterprise structure->Definition->Materials Management->Maintain Storage location

Created a SLOC called “AFS”, which is used in this document.

#2 Assign Warehouse number to Plant/Storage location

This configuration tells, whenever stock is posted to this Plant + Storage Location combination, the stock is physically managed in this EWM Warehouse Number.

SPRO->SCWM Extended warehouse management-> Extended warehouse management->Interfaces->Map storage locations from ERP system to EWM

 

#3 Define stock types

Stock Type defines the inventory status of a product in EWM. It controls what operations can be performed on the stock and how it is represented between ERP and EWM.

SPRO->SCWM Extended warehouse management-> Extended warehouse management->Goods Receipt Process->Configure availability group for put away->Configure stock type

 

#4 Storage type for Production supply area

In SAP EWM, a Storage Type for Production Supply Area (PSA) is the EWM storage type that represents the physical area where components are staged for production consumption. I am not showing other storage types but only showing PSA.

SPRO->SCWM Extended warehouse management-> Extended warehouse management->Master data->Define storage type

#5 Warehouse process type:

A Warehouse Process Type (WPT) controls how warehouse activities are executed in EWM. It determines the behavior of warehouse tasks such as putaway, picking, staging, internal movements, and production supply.

SPRO->SCWM Extended warehouse management-> Extended warehouse management->Cross process settings->Warehouse task->Define warehouse process type

WPT # 2010 is created for stock removal, especially for Staging process.

WPT # 3100 is created for replenishment, especially for crate parts.

#6 Determine warehouse process type:

This step is used to identify the warehouse process type depending upon the document type and Item type.

SPRO->SCWM Extended warehouse management-> Extended warehouse management->Cross process settings->Warehouse task->Determine warehouse process type

#7 Link SAP ERP order type to SAP EWM Document type:

This step link production order type to warehouse and document type.

SPRO->SCWM Extended warehouse management-> Extended warehouse management->Interfaces->ERP integration->Production->Map SAP ERP order type to SAP EWM Document type

#8 Link SAP ERP order type to SAP EWM Item type:

This step link component type with order type, warehouse, document type and item type.

SPRO->SCWM Extended warehouse management-> Extended warehouse management->Interfaces->ERP integration->Production->Map SAP ERP order type to SAP EWM item type

#9 Define Document types for production:

This is one of important step which assign action profile and other details to document type and category.

SPRO->SCWM Extended warehouse management-> Extended warehouse management->Goods Issue process->Production material request->Define document types for production

#10 Define item types for production:

This configuration step in SAP EWM that specifies the different item categories used in production integration documents, such as Production Material Requests (PMRs) and production supply processes. The item type controls how materials are processed, staged, consumed, and monitored during production activities

SPRO->SCWM Extended warehouse management-> Extended warehouse management->Goods Issue process->Production material request->Define item types for production

#11 Allowed Item types for production

This setting in SAP EWM that specifies which production item types can be used within a particular production process or document. It ensures that only valid item categories are processed for staging, consumption, and goods receipt activities.

SPRO->SCWM Extended warehouse management-> Extended warehouse management->Goods Issue process->Production material request->Allowed item types for production

#12 Customization delivery:

SCM Extended warehouse management->Extended Warehouse Management->Cross process Settings ->Delivery warehouse-request->Check Customising

Document type “OPC” is having below configuration details.

#13 Assign Storage type to search sequence:

Assign Storage Type to Search Sequence is a configuration step in SAP EWM that determines the order in which the system searches storage types when locating stock for warehouse activities such as picking, staging, or replenishment.

Storage type “0011” wherein we have a component stock.

SPRO->SCWM Extended warehouse management-> Extended warehouse management->Goods Issue process->Specify storage type search sequence

#14 Storage type search sequence:

This configuration in SAP EWM that defines the order in which the system searches storage types to find stock for warehouse processes such as picking, staging, replenishment, and internal movements. EWM checks each storage type in the defined sequence until sufficient stock is found.

SPRO->SCWM Extended warehouse management-> Extended warehouse management->Goods Issue process->Determine Specify storage type search sequence for stock removal

#15 Activate Replenishment Strategies in Storage Types:

Activate Replenishment Strategies in Storage Types is a configuration setting in SAP EWM that enables automatic replenishment of stock within a storage type when inventory falls below a predefined threshold. This ensures that sufficient inventory is always available to support warehouse operations and production processes without manual intervention.

SPRO->SCWM Extended warehouse management-> Extended warehouse management->Internal warehouse processes->Replenishment control->Activate replenishment strategies in storage type

Master data:

#1 Map the EWM PSA to the ERP PSA(/SCWM/PSAMAP):

This step that links the Production Supply Area (PSA) defined in SAP EWM with the corresponding PSA in ERP/PP. This mapping enables seamless integration between production and warehouse processes, allowing EWM to stage components to the correct production area based on Production Material Requests (PMRs). It ensures that materials are delivered to the appropriate location for production consumption.

#2 Work Center (CR01):

A Work Center in SAP PP represents the physical location, machine, production line, or group of resources where manufacturing operations are performed. It is used in routings and production orders to define where an operation will be executed and to provide scheduling, capacity planning, and costing information. In SAP EWM production integration, the work center can also be linked to a Production Supply Area (PSA) to support component staging and consumption processes.

#3 Created BOM(CS01):

A Bill of Material (BOM) is a structured list of all raw materials, components, semi-finished products, and quantities required to manufacture a finished product. It defines what materials are needed for production and in what quantity. During production order creation, SAP uses the BOM to determine component requirements, generate reservations, and create Production Material Requests (PMRs) for staging in SAP EWM.

Material # 1001590 is finish product and having three components (1001575, 1001596, 1001597) and made below BOM.

#4 Component stock availability(/SCWM/MON):

Mentioned component stocks are available for staging and consumptions.

#5 Creation of Routing (CA01):

A Routing is a master data object in SAP PP that defines the sequence of operations required to manufacture a product. It specifies the work center, operation steps, processing times, and production activities needed to complete the manufacturing process. During production order creation, SAP uses the routing for scheduling, capacity planning, and determining where components staged from SAP EWM will be consumed.

Created routing for finish product # 1001590.

#6 Control Cycle (LPK1):

A Control Cycle is a master data object in SAP PP that defines how materials are supplied from the warehouse to a Production Supply Area (PSA) for manufacturing. For Material # 1001575, a control cycle was created to define how the material is supplied from the warehouse to the production area. The EWM Staging Indicator (5) specifies that the material will be supplied using Production Material Request (PMR)-based staging in SAP EWM. During production order creation, EWM automatically generates a PMR and stages the required components to the designated Production Supply Area (PSA) based on the control cycle settings.

 

 

Below control Cycle for material #1001596, defines how a material is supplied from the warehouse to the Production Supply Area (PSA). It links the material, work center/PSA, warehouse, and staging method used for production supply. Staging Indicator = 2 (Crate Part Staging). Crate Part Staging means EWM maintains a fixed number of containers (crates) in the PSA. The system monitors the quantity available in the PSA and automatically replenishes the crates when stock falls below the required level.

 

Below control cycle for material # 1001597, with Manual Staging, SAP EWM does not automatically create replenishment requests or stage materials to the PSA. The warehouse user must manually trigger the staging process and move the required quantity from the warehouse to the PSA bin.

 

#7 Assign Bin to Product/Entitled in PSA for EWM(/SCWM/PSASTAGE):

Assign Bin to Product/Entitled in PSA for EWM is a step that defines the specific PSA storage bin where a particular product will be staged for production. It links the product (and, if applicable, the entitled party) to a designated PSA bin, ensuring that SAP EWM delivers components to the correct production supply location.

#8 Product Master(/SCWM/MAT1):

Product master is created with stock removal control as “PICK”

End to End Transactions:

#1 Production order (CO01):

Below production order is created for finish material # 1001590. And all three components are assign to operation # 10.

#2 Staging for Single order release parts (CO02):

Control cycle for material # 1001575 was created as EWM staging and single order release.

#3 PMR display(/SCWM/MON):

After material staging step, below PMR was created for material # 1001575.

#4 Creation of staging task(/SCWM/STAGE):

Staging task was created for material # 1001575, because this material is linked with EWM staging.

 

#5 Warehouse task display(/SCWM/MON):

Warehouse task # 100068 created for material # 1001575 to move the stock(staging) from 0011(normal storage type) to 1005(PSA storage type) + specific bin # PSA1-1005-BIN1

#6 Replenishment for the crate part(/SCWM/REPL):

This replenishment task is created for material # 1001596, as control cycle for this material is created as Crate part staging.

Warehouse Task # 100069(/SCWM/MON):

#7 Manual staging(/SCWM/ADPROD):

Manual staging can be done by /SCWM/RFUI(Scanner) or /SCWM/ADPROD(GUI). As in this example, I am using /SCWM/ADPROD.

 

#8 Summarized Warehouse tasks(/SCWM/MON):

Below three warehouse tasks are created for all three components.

#9 Confirm Warehouse tasks(/SCWM/MON):

Warehouse task can be confirmed by /SCWM/RFUI(Scanner) or /SCWM/MON(GUI), however I am confirming using /SCWM/MON.

#10 Stock status in PSA bins (/SCWM/MON):

After warehouse tasks confirmation, stock is correctly populated in PSA bins.

#11 Consumption of components against Production order (/SCWM/RFUI):

First component # 1001575 was consumed using manufacturing order # 1001345.

#12 Consumption of components using CO15:

Remaining two components (#1001596, 1001597) are consumed using production order confirmation.

 

#13 Outbound delivery order((/SCWM/PRDO)

After production order confirmation, below outbound delivery order was created

#14 Outbound delivery(/SCWM/PRDO):

After post goods issue, below outbound delivery was created.

#15 Goods receipt of Finish material:

After Production order confirmation ERP Inbound delivery # 180109341 was created automatically and as a next step Inbound delivery # 325 was created in EWM automatically.

#16 Warehouse task(/SCWM/MON):

Below warehouse task was confirmed and now stock is available.

Conclusion:

The integration of SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM) and Production Planning (PP) in SAP S/4HANA provides a robust framework for synchronizing warehouse and manufacturing operations. By connecting production orders with warehouse execution processes, organizations can ensure timely material availability, improve inventory accuracy, and enhance overall production efficiency.

Through key integration components such as Production Material Requests (PMRs), Production Supply Areas (PSAs), material staging, warehouse task processing, production confirmations, and finished goods receipt management, SAP enables a seamless flow of information and materials across the manufacturing environment. This integration eliminates manual interventions, increases process visibility, and supports real-time inventory updates throughout the production lifecycle.

By implementing the configuration and process steps described in this article, businesses can establish a tightly integrated production supply chain that supports efficient manufacturing execution, accurate inventory management, and enhanced warehouse productivity within SAP S/4HANA.

 Author: Naveen Chandra Sharma

(Naveen is SAP Solution architect having 29+ years of experience which includes 20+ years into SAP consulting with expertise in SAP ECC, SAP S/4HANA and integration with other external system like MES, CIMCORP etc. He worked on various type of project flavors like Implementation, Enhancement and support projects in various industries Manufacturing, Finance, Pharmaceutical, Food, Telecommunication)

entry.title: SAP EWM DODO — The 4 Variants You Must Know Before You Configure (DODO in Embedded S/4HANA 2025 EWM)
entry.content:
This blog is based on hands-on configuration and troubleshooting of DODO in an S/4HANA 2025 embedded EWM sandbox. All findings are from direct sandbox work — not copied from SAP Help. Screenshots are from warehouse EPG1 on S/4HANA 2025 FPS01.

Objective

The purpose of this blog is to present a practical framework for SAP EWM Direct Outbound Delivery Order (DODO) in embedded S/4HANA 2025. DODO allows creating an Outbound Delivery Order (ODO) directly in EWM without a preceding ERP delivery — EWM is the source, and S/4HANA creates the corresponding document dynamically.

Many consultants — myself included — have spent hours debugging DODO only to discover they were configuring the wrong variant entirely. This blog presents a clear 4-variant framework to help you choose the right path before you configure, and a diagnostic guide for the most common blocker: the IS_HEADER_ORG empty payload in SMQ2.

 

Background — What Is DODO?

In the standard SAP outbound flow, the sequence is: S/4HANA creates a sales order → creates an outbound delivery → distributes it to EWM → EWM executes the delivery. DODO reverses the first step: EWM creates the ODO first, and S/4HANA creates the corresponding ERP document dynamically via qRFC.

This is particularly useful in scenarios where the warehouse initiates the outbound process — for example, directly loading a production output pallet onto a shuttle truck to another plant, without waiting for an ERP-initiated delivery.

 

Section 1: The 4 DODO Variants

After researching 5 sources (SAP Help, two SAP Community blogs, an SAP Press project config guide, and direct sandbox work), I identified four distinct DODO variants in embedded S/4HANA 2025. Each has a different document type, ERP communication path, and function module called in the background.

Choosing the wrong variant is the #1 reason DODO implementations fail. The document type (OPIG vs ODSH), the ship-to partner type (customer BP vs plant BP), and the Comm. to ERP setting on the document type all determine which ERP path is followed. These are not interchangeable.

 

Variant

Doc Type

Item Type

Ship-To Partner

FM Called in Background

Use Case

1 — Customer Pickup

OPIG

ODPG

Customer BP (external)

/ SPE/OUTB_DELIVERY_SAVEREPLICA

External customer collects from warehouse without prior sales order

2 — Direct STO via OPIG

OPIG

ODPG

Plant BP (T001W.KUNNR)

/ SPE/CREATE_STO_FOR_DIRDLV + /SPE/DELIVERY_CREATE_FROM_STO

Plant-to-plant STO — SAP recommended path for embedded S/4HANA

3 — Direct STO via ODSH

ODSH

ODSH

Plant BP

/ SPE/CREATE_STO_FOR_DIRDLV + /SPE/DELIVERY_CREATE_FROM_STO

Plant-to-plant STO via ODSH doc type — requires specific Comm. to ERP setting

4 — Goods Movement Only

ODSH

ODSH

Not required

/ SCWM/ERP_GM_SYNC_UPD_S4 + /SPE/GOODSMVT_CREATE

Scrapping, cost center issue, internal consumption — no ERP delivery created

 

Let me walk through each variant in detail.

 

Variant 1: OPIG — Customer Pickup (Standard Customer Delivery)

This is the classic DODO scenario. The warehouse creates an ODO using document type OPIG (Outbound Delivery Order for Pickup) and the ship-to party is an external customer BP.

The key setting on OPIG that distinguishes it from ODSH is: Pickup = A (Mandatory). This flag tells EWM that someone is coming to collect — and when combined with a customer BP as ship-to, routes the replication to /SPE/OUTB_DELIVERY_SAVEREPLICA, which creates a standard LE outbound delivery in S/4HANA.

 

 

 

The system flow:

  • /SCWM/PRDO: Create ODO with OPIG/ODPG, ship-to = customer BP, product + WPT
  • EWM creates warehouse order and warehouse task for picking
  • qRFC message sent to S/4HANA: /SPE/OUTB_DELIVERY_SAVEREPLICA with IS_HEADER_ORG (Sales Org/DC/Div/ShPt)
  • S/4HANA creates LE outbound delivery (type LF) — visible in VL03N
  • Pick, confirm WT, post GI in EWM → movement type 601 in S/4HANA

 

Variant 2: OPIG — Direct STO (SAP Recommended Path for Plant-to-Plant Transfer)

This is the SAP-recommended path for EWM-triggered plant-to-plant stock transfers in embedded S/4HANA. It uses the same OPIG document type as Variant 1, but the ship-to party is a plant BP (the customer number assigned to the receiving plant in T001W.KUNNR) — not an external customer.

When EWM detects a plant BP as ship-to on an OPIG ODO (combined with the Pickup = Mandatory flag), it routes the replication via the STO function modules instead of the customer delivery FM. This is the architectural difference  not the document type, but the partner type.

Variant 2 is the path described in SAP's own blog 'Advanced Supply Chains with SAP EWM — Direct Loading of Finished Goods.' If you are implementing a plant-to-plant DODO in embedded S/4HANA 2025, start here.

 

The system flow:

  • /SCWM/PRDO: Create ODO with OPIG/ODPG, ship-to = receiving plant BP (T001W.KUNNR), assign HU at loading
  • EWM detects plant BP → routes to STO FMs
  • qRFC: /SPE/CREATE_STO_FOR_DIRDLV → creates STO Purchase Order (type UB) in S/4HANA
  • qRFC: /SPE/DELIVERY_CREATE_FROM_STO → creates NL outbound delivery linked to STO PO
  • NL delivery distributed back to EWM for execution
  • Post GI in EWM → movement type 641 (GI for STO with delivery) in S/4HANA
  • SPED output triggers automatic inbound delivery at receiving plant

 

Variant 3: ODSH — Direct STO via ODSH Document Type

Variant 3: ODSH — Direct STO via ODSH Document Type

This variant uses ODSH (ODO Direct Outbound Delivery) instead of OPIG for the STO flow. It requires the /SPE/DIRDLV_TYPE table to have an entry with Doc.Cat = J (Stock Transfer) for the ODSH delivery type.

S/4HANA 2025 FPS01 sandbox — confirmed working (June 2026):

Initial testing suggested this variant was blocked because the standard ODSH 'Comm. to ERP' field did not appear to expose an STO routing option, and the system was calling /SPE/OUTB_DELIVERY_SAVEREPLICA instead of /SPE/CREATE_STO_FOR_DIRDLV. Following feedback from Aleš Knor (SAP Community Explorer, running Variant 3 in production on S/4HANA 2023), the sandbox was retested with the /SPE/DIRDLV_TYPE entry correctly configured with Doc.Cat = J.

Result: STO and Outbound Delivery created successfully in S/4HANA 2025 via the same qRFC path as Variant 2. GI confirmed end-to-end. The earlier finding was a configuration gap, not a platform limitation.

The critical config prerequisite: the /SPE/DIRDLV_TYPE table must have a correctly configured entry for ODSH with Doc.Cat = J. Without this entry the system cannot route to /SPE/CREATE_STO_FOR_DIRDLV regardless of the Comm. to ERP setting on the document type.

Variant 3 vs Variant 2: both are now confirmed working in S/4HANA 2025 FPS01 for plant-to-plant DODO. Variant 2 (OPIG + plant BP) is SAP's documented recommended path. Variant 3 (ODSH) is a valid alternative where ODSH is already in use in the implementation.

Variant 4: ODSH — Goods Movement Only (No ERP Delivery)

This variant uses ODSH with ‘Comm. to ERP = B (Via Goods Movement Interface)’. No LE delivery or STO is created in S/4HANA — stock is moved via a direct goods movement posting. This is the intended path for scrapping, cost center issues, internal consumption, and VAS component reservations.

 

 

The system flow:

  • /SCWM/PRDO: Create ODO with ODSH, Comm. to ERP = B, product + WPT (no ship-to customer needed)
  • EWM creates WO + WT, pick and confirm
  • Post GI in EWM
  • Synchronous goods movement: /SCWM/ERP_GM_SYNC_UPD_S4 + /SPE/GOODSMVT_CREATE
  • Material document created in S/4HANA — no delivery — verify in MB51/MIGO

S/4HANA 2025 FPS01 sandbox observation: When creating a custom document type (ZDSH) by copying the standard ODSH, the Comm. to ERP field was greyed out and could not be changed to “B — Via Goods Movement Interface.” The root cause was not fully investigated in this session. The config path above documents the intended SAP design.

Variant 4 vs /SCWM/ADGI — when does ODSH add value?

A natural question: if /SCWM/ADGI already handles scrapping directly in EWM, why configure Variant 4 at all? For pure scrapping with no process control requirements, /SCWM/ADGI is the simpler and recommended path. However, ODSH-based Variant 4 provides architectural value in three specific scenarios: (1) Process control and workflow — ODSH creates a formal ODO with warehouse order and task, allowing a physical confirmation step before the goods movement. /SCWM/ADGI posts directly with no WT/WO. Where supervisor approval or quality hold release is required before scrapping, ODSH is the right design. (2) HU-managed stock with lifecycle requirements — where a sealed HU must be formally deactivated as part of the goods movement, the ODSH warehouse task flow handles HU lifecycle correctly. (3) Audit trail and document traceability — the ODO-to-material-document chain provides granular traceability that /SCWM/ADGI does not, which matters in pharma, food, and medical device implementations. If none of these apply, use /SCWM/ADGI.

 

Section 2: Key Configuration Steps

Below is a consolidated list of the key configuration steps required across Variants 1 and 2 (OPIG/ODPG path). Variant 4 requires only the ODSH document type change.

2.1 EWM: Define Document Type OPIG

IMG: SCM Extended Warehouse Management > EWM > Goods Issue Process > Outbound Delivery > Define Document Types for Outbound Delivery Process

Field

Value

Note

Document Type

OPIG

Standard SAP — do not copy unless required

Pickup

A — Mandatory

KEY FIELD — distinguishes OPIG from ODSH. Triggers STO routing when plant BP is ship-to.

Comm. to ERP

System Default

Do not change for Variants 1 and 2

Process Profile

/SCWM/OUT_PRD_PICKUP

Standard pickup profile

2.2 S/4HANA: Delivery Type Determination for DODO

Table: /SPE/DIRDLV_TYPE

Field

Value

Doc.Cat.

J (Stock Transfer)

EWM Del. Type

* (wildcard)

Initiator

* (wildcard)

Warehouse

Your warehouse number

Del. type

ODSH

IC DlvTy

NL (intra-company)

CC DlvTy

NLCC (cross-company)

2.3 S/4HANA: Item Category Determination

Table: /SPE/DIRITM_CATG

Field

Value

EWM Doc Type / Item Type

ODSH / ODSH

IC ReplItC

NLN (intra-company)

CC ReplItC

NLC (cross-company)

2.4 S/4HANA: STO Setup (Variant 2 specific)

IMG: MM > Purchasing > Purchase Order > Set Up Stock Transport Order

  • Document type: UB
  • Supplying plant: your sending plant
  • Delivery type: NL
  • Item category: NLN

2.5 Master Data: Receiving Plant as Customer

  • T001W: CustNoPlnt = plant customer number for receiving plant
  • T001W: SO ICB = sending plant sales org (critical for IS_HEADER_ORG derivation)
  • T001W: VSTEL = shipping point for sending plant. CRITICAL: this field is populated via SPRO → Logistics General → Handling Unit Management → Basics → Materials Management → Inventory Management → HU Requirement for Storage Locations and Default Values for Deliveries. Per SAP Note 2698417: OVXC does NOT populate T001W.VSTEL (OVXC is for shipping point determination in sales orders only). Transaction OMGN does not exist in S/4HANA. See Section 3 for full diagnostic detail — this is the most commonly missed field in all DODO implementations.
  • KNVV: Extend plant customer to sending plant sales area (VKORG/VTWEG/SPART)

 

Section 3: The Most Common Blocker — IS_HEADER_ORG Empty in SMQ2

If you have configured DODO and are getting error VP201 'Customer not maintained for sales area' in SMQ2, the first thing to check is NOT the customer master. Check the IS_HEADER_ORG parameter in the SMQ2 queue container display.

SMQ2 — Inbound Queue showing /SPE/OUTB_DELIVERY_SAVEREPLICA with VP201 error: “Customer 316 not maintained for sales area”

 

IS_HEADER_ORG is the import parameter of /SPE/OUTB_DELIVERY_SAVEREPLICA that contains the organizational unit data for the ERP delivery: Shipping Point (ShPt), Sales Org (SOrg), Distribution Channel (DC), Division (Div), and Warehouse Number (WhN).

When IS_HEADER_ORG is empty, the system cannot determine which sales area to use for the delivery, and VP201 fires regardless of whether the customer is correctly extended to a sales area. The customer master is not the problem — the derivation logic upstream is the problem.

Do not spend time extending customers to additional sales areas when IS_HEADER_ORG is blank. Fix the derivation first.

The Fix: How to Correctly Populate T001W.VSTEL

This is the most misunderstood step in DODO configuration. Two commonly referenced transactions do NOT work:

OMGN — does not exist in S/4HANA (ECC-era transaction)

OVXC — assigns shipping points for shipping point determination in sales orders. Per SAP Note 2698417, OVXC does NOT populate T001W.VSTEL. Many consultants waste hours here.

The correct path (confirmed in S/4HANA 2025 FPS01):

SPRO → Logistics General → Handling Unit Management →

Basics → Materials Management → Inventory Management →

HU Requirement for Storage Locations and Default Values for Deliveries

In this screen, select your supplying plant (PG11 in my sandbox) and assign the relevant shipping point (PGS1). This writes directly to T001W.VSTEL. Once saved, run /$SYNC, delete any stuck SMQ2 entry, create a fresh ODO — IS_HEADER_ORG will now populate with the shipping point.

IS_HEADER_ORG Root Cause Hierarchy (ranked by likelihood)

Priority

Root cause

Check

Fix

1 — MOST LIKELY

Shipping Point (VSTEL) blank for sending plant in T001W

SE16N > T001W > sending plant > field VSTEL

Assign shipping point via: SPRO > LE > Shipping > Shipping Points > Assign Shipping Points to Plants. Use TVSTZ table to find valid shipping points for the plant.

2

Sales Org not assigned to sending plant

SPRO > Enterprise Structure > Assignment > SD > Assign Sales Org - DC - Plant

Add entry for your sales org/DC → sending plant combination

3

Old SMQ2 queue entry — config changed after creation

Check FM name in SMQ2 queue container display — still /SPE/OUTB_DELIVERY_SAVEREPLICA?

Delete old queue entry + delete old ODO + /$SYNC + create fresh ODO. Queue entries are frozen at creation time — they replay original routing.

4

OPIG doc type missing sales area context

IS_HEADER_DATA.DlvTy in SMQ2 — what delivery type is the system trying to create?

Verify /SPE/DIRDLV_TYPE entry for your warehouse exists with correct Doc.Cat

 

Queue Container Display — IS_HEADER_ORG highlighted (yellow), right panel showing all fields empty: Delivery | ShPt | SOrg. | LoadPt | SOff. | WhN | SDst | Door | Unload.pt.

 

Section 4: EWM Warehouse Task Creation — Independent of ERP Replication

An important observation from sandbox testing: the EWM side of DODO (ODO creation, warehouse order, warehouse task, staging) works correctly even when the ERP replication is blocked in SMQ2.

This means you can validate and test all EWM-side DODO configuration (storage type, WPT determination, bin selection, warehouse task creation) before resolving the ERP replication blocker. The EWM and ERP sides can be debugged independently.

In my sandbox, Warehouse Order 3000003721 and Warehouse Task 1000006896 were successfully created against the ODO with:

  • Process Type: 2010 (Stock Removal)
  • Source Storage Type: DODO (staging area)
  • Source Bin: DD1
  • Destination Storage Type: Y920
  • Destination Bin: ST03 (staging bay for truck loading)
  • WO Creation Rule: DODO

 

Section 5: Configuration Summary Table

Config item

Variant 1

Variant 2

Variant 3

Variant 4

EWM Doc Type

OPIG

OPIG

ODSH

ODSH

Ship-to partner

Customer BP

Plant BP

Plant BP

Not required

OPIG Pickup setting

A — Mandatory

A — Mandatory

N/A

N/A

ODSH Comm. to ERP

N/A

N/A

System Default

B — Via Goods Mvmt

/SPE/DIRDLV_TYPE

Doc.Cat=J required

Doc.Cat=J required

Doc.Cat=J required

Not required

STO setup (UB/NL)

Not required

Required

Required

Not required

T001W.VSTEL (ShPt)

Required

Required

Required

Not required

KNVV sales area extension

Required

Required for plant customer

Required for plant customer

Not required

FM called

OUTB_DELIVERY_SAVEREPLICA

CREATE_STO_FOR_DIRDLV

CREATE_STO_FOR_DIRDLV (if working)

ERP_GM_SYNC_UPD_S4

ERP document created

LE Outbound Delivery (LF)

STO PO (UB) + NL Delivery

STO PO (UB) + NL Delivery

Material Document only

S/4HANA 2025 status

Standard — confirmed

SAP recommended — confirmed

Blocked in standard ODSH

S/4HANA 2025 FPS01: Comm. to ERP field greyed out on copied ZDSH doc type — field lock root cause not investigated. /SCWM/ADGI recommended as practical alternative. Architectural value of ODSH documented for process control, HU lifecycle, and audit trail scenarios.

 

Conclusion

DODO is not a single process — it has four distinct variants, each with different document types, partner configurations, ERP communication paths, and function modules. Choosing the wrong variant before configuring is the single most common reason DODO implementations stall.

The key takeaways from this blog:

  • For plant-to-plant STO in embedded S/4HANA 2025: use Variant 2 (OPIG + plant BP). This is SAP's recommended path.
  • When IS_HEADER_ORG is empty in SMQ2: check T001W.VSTEL (Shipping Point) for the sending plant first — this is the most commonly missed field.
  • Always create a fresh ODO after SPRO config changes — old queue entries replay original routing regardless of what was changed.
  • EWM warehouse task creation is independent of ERP replication — validate EWM-side config before chasing the SMQ2 blocker.

For OPIG-based DODO (Variants 1 and 2): Picking status “Not Relevant” is by design — not a misconfiguration. GI must always be initiated from EWM (/SCWM/PRDO), never from VL02N in S/4HANA.

For internal goods movements without process control requirements: /SCWM/ADGI is simpler and sufficient. Use Variant 4 (ODSH) when you need a formal warehouse task confirmation step, HU lifecycle management, or a complete document audit trail — and first verify Comm. to ERP field availability in your system version.

 

I hope this blog saves you the hours of debugging it took me to arrive at this framework. Please leave a comment if you have encountered a DODO variant not covered here, if you have found a working path for Variant 3 (ODSH STO) in S/4HANA 2025, or if you have successfully configured Variant 4 with Comm. to ERP = B in S/4HANA 2025 — I would be keen to understand the field lock behaviour others have seen.

 

Happy Learning!

Yogesh Agarwal

Enterprise Logistics Execution Specialist | SAP EWM & TM | Warehouse Transformation | SAP MM/LE Consultant | MSCG | S/4HANA 2025 EWM

LinkedIn: linkedin.com/in/ayogesh.

entry.title: SAP EWM - Smart Way to Prevent Putaway for Delivery on Failed QRFC Queues
entry.content:
Business Scenario

In SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM), when we post a Goods Receipt (GR), any subsequent queue failure in SMQ1 (Outbound Queue) or SMQ2 (Inbound Queue) prevents the GR data from being communicated back to the ERP system.

Quite often, this goes unnoticed because users assume the system will process the GR in ERP without errors. As a result, warehouse tasks (WT) for the putaway are created (either manually or automatically) and subsequently confirmed. While this correctly updates the physical and logical stock in EWM, the ERP system remains un-updated, leading to critical inventory mismatches between the two systems.

The Challenge & Solution

I recently worked on a business requirement where the client faced significant operational challenges in identifying and resolving these queue errors. A major pain point was that once the putaway process is fully completed, reversing the transactions to fix the queue becomes highly complex or restricted.

To proactively resolve this issue, I implemented a custom enhancement during the Warehouse Task Creation stage. This enhancement automatically intercepts and aborts the WT creation if it detects a failed QRFC queue tied to that specific inbound delivery. Catching the error at this initial stage prevents the mismatched putaway from happening in the first place, drastically reducing inventory discrepancies and manual cleanup efforts.

Configuration: 
To make GR posting as mandatory first step and user should not directly go and create WT for delivery. Even if user try to create WT bypassing GR, Checkbox for WT after GR will ensure user to post GR first and then create WT.

Go to /SCWM/PRDI.  Select your delivery and post GR. Goods Receipt will be completed in EWM.  

In ERP, Goods movement status is still 'A' and a Queue is stuck in SMQ2 with an error. 

Now if we try to create a WT, for this EWM will not stop us and it can create WT to move product to final destination. 

We can save and confirm Warehouse order and Warehouse task. But since there is already a failed queue in SMQ2, communication will not happen to ERP and there will be inventory difference in two system. 

BADI /SCWM/EX_CORE_CR_ABORT can be implemented to add the additional logic for this business case. 

An implementation is created for BADI and activated. Below logic is added in a method ABORT which will be called during WT creation. 

Set a Breakpoint in a method and check while creating warehouse task. We can observe since the queue is stuck for a delivery in SMQ2, BADI is called during WT creation, and it is adding log + aborting Putaway warehouse task creation throwing error which will make aware user to resolve the queue error first. 

Putaway WT not created for delivery.

 

Conclusion: Using BADI /SCWM/EX_CORE_CR_ABORT we can abort WT creation for putaway if there is a failed queue. Above code can be structure and modified as per different scenarios and requirements and thus can be explored more.  

Note: All testings are performed on S4HANA 2023.
